request.question: Who published the comic book in which Jean Loring was a supporting character and primary love interest of The Atom (Dr. Ray Palmer) is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books?

title: Rumiko Fujikawa
Jean Loring
Barbara Gordon
Lois Lane
Atom (Ray Palmer)
Snapper Carr
Spider-Man
Superman
Nova (Richard Rider)
Valkyrie (Marvel Comics)
sentences: Rumiko Fujikawa is a fictional character, a supporting character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.
Created by writer Kurt Busiek and artist Sean Chen, the character made her first appearance in "The Invincible Iron Man" vol.
A supporting character of Iron Man, she was Tony's primary love interest throughout the duration of the series.
Jean Loring is a fictional character in comic books published by DC Comics, formerly associated with superhero the Atom for whom she was a supporting character and primary love interest.
She first appeared in "Showcase" #34 (September–October 1961), created by Gardner Fox and Gil Kane.
The character appeared continually in minor roles until the 2004 storyline "Identity Crisis", in which she suffered a mental breakdown and murdered Sue Dibny, wife of the Elongated Man.
This would later lead Loring to assume the mantle of the supervillain Eclipso.
Barbara Gordon is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics, commonly in association with the superhero Batman.
The character was created by William Dozier, Julius Schwartz, and Carmine Infantino.

At the request of the producers of the 1960s "Batman" television series, DC editor Schwartz called for a new female counterpart to the superhero Batman that could be introduced into publication and the third season of the show simultaneously.
The character subsequently made her first comic book appearance as Batgirl in "Detective Comics" #359, titled "The Million Dollar Debut of Batgirl!"
(January 1967), by writer Gardner Fox and artist Carmine Infantino.
Lois Lane is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.
Created by writer Jerry Siegel and artist Joe Shuster, she first appeared in "Action Comics" #1 (June 1938).
Lois is a reporter for the Metropolis newspaper, the "Daily Planet", an award-winning journalist and the primary love interest of the superhero Superman.
The Atom (Dr. Ray Palmer) is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.
The character was created by editor and co-plotter Julius Schwartz, writer Gardner Fox and penciler Gil Kane.
The Atom was one of the first superheroes of the Silver Age of comic books and debuted in "Showcase" #34 (October 1961).
Lucas "Snapper" Carr is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.

The character, whose fictional nickname is almost always used by other characters in favor of his given name, was created by Gardner Fox (writer) and Mike Sekowsky (penciller), and made his first appearance in "The Brave and the Bold" in February 1960.
From 1960 to 1969, Snapper Carr appeared as a supporting character to the Justice League of America, a superhero team.
The character occasionally appeared in comics featuring the Justice League from 1969 to 1989, when the "Invasion!
" limited-series comic book gave him superpowers.
He was associated with a new superhero team, The Blasters, in various comics until 1993, when he lost his powers and became a main character in the "Hourman" comic book.
After the cancellation of "Hourman" in April 2001, he became a main character in the "Young Justice" comic book beginning in December 2001.

"Young Justice" was cancelled in May 2003, and he became associated with the governmental organization Checkmate, a role revealed when the character played a small but important role in the 2007-2008 limited series comic book "52 Aftermath: The Four Horsemen".
The character made major appearances in "Final Crisis: Resist" in December 2008 and "Justice League of America 80-Page Giant" in November 2009.
Spider-Man is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.
The character was created by writer-editor Stan Lee and writer-artist Steve Ditko, and first appeared in the anthology comic book "Amazing Fantasy" #15 (August 1962) in the Silver Age of Comic Books.

Lee and Ditko conceived the character as an orphan being raised by his Aunt May and Uncle Ben in New York City after his parents Richard and Mary Parker were killed in a plane crash, and as a teenager, having to deal with the normal struggles of adolescence in addition to those of a costumed crime-fighter.

Spider-Man's creators gave him super strength and agility, the ability to cling to most surfaces, shoot spider-webs using wrist-mounted devices of his own invention, which he calls "web-shooters", and react to danger quickly with his "spider-sense", enabling him to combat his foes.
Superman is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.
The character was created by writer Jerry Siegel and artist Joe Shuster, high school students living in Cleveland, Ohio, in 1933.
They sold Superman to Detective Comics, the future DC Comics, in 1938.
Superman debuted in "Action Comics" #1 (cover-dated June 1938) and subsequently appeared in various radio serials, newspaper strips, television programs, films, and video games.
With this success, Superman helped to create the superhero archetype and establish its primacy within the American comic book.
The character is also referred to by such epithets as the Big Blue Boy Scout, the Man of Steel, the Man of Tomorrow, and the Last Son of Krypton.
Nova (Richard Rider) is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.
The character appeared historically as the star of his own series, and at other times, as a supporting character in team books such as "The New Warriors".
He is a member of the intergalactic police force known as the Nova Corps, for which he gained superhuman abilities including enhanced strength, flight and resistance to injury.
Valkyrie is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.
The character was created by Roy Thomas and John Buscema, based on the Norse mythological figure Brynhildr.
The character first appeared in "The Avengers" #83 (December 1970) and has become a mainstay of the superhero team known as the Defenders.
An Asgardian by birth, Valkyrie, also known by her real name, Brunnhilde, was selected by Odin to lead the Valkyrior.
Among her other aliases are Barbara Denton-Norriss, Samantha Parrington, Sian Bowen, and Annabelle Riggs who were all host bodies to the spirit of Brunnhilde.
Samantha Parrington, a previous host of Brunnhilde, later received Valkyrie's powers and became a member of the Defenders herself.
An ally and one-time love interest of Thor, she was ranked 30th in IGN's list of "The Top 50 Avengers", and 65th in "Comics Buyer's Guide's" "100 Sexiest Women in Comics" list.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Who published the comic book in which Jean Loring was a supporting character and primary love interest of The Atom (Dr. Ray Palmer) is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books?
hop1_docs: Jean Loring | Jean Loring is a fictional character in comic books published by DC Comics, formerly associated with superhero the Atom for whom she was a supporting character and primary love interest.  She first appeared in "Showcase" #34 (September–October 1961), created by Gardner Fox and Gil Kane.  The character appeared continually in minor roles until the 2004 storyline "Identity Crisis", in which she suffered a mental breakdown and murdered Sue Dibny, wife of the Elongated Man.  This would later lead Loring to assume the mantle of the supervillain Eclipso.

Atom (Ray Palmer) | The Atom (Dr. Ray Palmer) is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.  The character was created by editor and co-plotter Julius Schwartz, writer Gardner Fox and penciler Gil Kane.  The Atom was one of the first superheroes of the Silver Age of comic books and debuted in "Showcase" #34 (October 1961).

Lois Lane | Lois Lane is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.  Created by writer Jerry Siegel and artist Joe Shuster, she first appeared in "Action Comics" #1 (June 1938).  Lois is a reporter for the Metropolis newspaper, the "Daily Planet", an award-winning journalist and the primary love interest of the superhero Superman.

Rumiko Fujikawa | Rumiko Fujikawa is a fictional character, a supporting character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.  Created by writer Kurt Busiek and artist Sean Chen, the character made her first appearance in "The Invincible Iron Man" vol.  3 #4 (May 1998).  A supporting character of Iron Man, she was Tony's primary love interest throughout the duration of the series.

Elektra (comics) | Elektra Natchios ( , ) is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.  Created by Frank Miller, the character first appeared in "Daredevil" #168 (January 1981).  She is a love interest of the superhero Daredevil, but her violent nature and mercenary lifestyle divide the two.

Lucius Fox | Lucius Fox is a fictional character appearing American comic books published by DC Comics, commonly in association with the superhero Batman.  As a supporting character in the comics, he acts as Bruce Wayne's business manager at Wayne Enterprises who runs the business interests that supply Batman's equipment needs as well as financing his operations.

Claire Temple | Claire Temple is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.  An African-American doctor, she is primarily affiliated with the superhero Luke Cage and is one of his early love interests.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Jean Loring — [1]: "comic books published by DC Comics"; "supporting character and primary love interest"
- The Atom (Dr. Ray Palmer) — [2]: "fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics."

Ambiguities:
- None; both passages unambiguously attribute the comics to "DC Comics."

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing facts; the publisher is explicitly given as "DC Comics" in the provided passages. Final conclusion: publisher = DC Comics.

Retrieval direction:
- Optional verification pages: "Jean Loring" and "Atom (Ray Palmer)" (Wikipedia pages or equivalent). 
- Optional search queries: "Jean Loring DC Comics publisher" and "Atom (Ray Palmer) DC Comics publisher".
- No deferral — final answer can be given: DC Comics.
hop2_docs: Jean Loring | Jean Loring is a fictional character in comic books published by DC Comics, formerly associated with superhero the Atom for whom she was a supporting character and primary love interest.  She first appeared in "Showcase" #34 (September–October 1961), created by Gardner Fox and Gil Kane.  The character appeared continually in minor roles until the 2004 storyline "Identity Crisis", in which she suffered a mental breakdown and murdered Sue Dibny, wife of the Elongated Man.  This would later lead Loring to assume the mantle of the supervillain Eclipso.

List of DC Comics imprint publications | DC Comics is one of the largest comic book and graphic novel publishers in North America.  DC has published comic books under a number of different imprints and corporate names.  This page lists all series, mini-series, limited series, and graphic novels published under the imprints All-Star, ABC, CMX, DC Focus, Helix, Homage, Impact, Johnny DC, Milestone, Minx, Paradox Press, Piranha Press, Tangent, Vertigo, WildStorm, and those Amalgam Comics published by DC.

List of DC Comics publications | DC Comics is one of the largest comic book publishers in North America.  DC has published comic books under a number of different imprints and corporate names.  This is a list of all series, mini-series, limited series, and comic book sized one-shots published under the imprints DC or AA, and published by National Periodical Publications, National Comics Publications, All-American Comics, Inc., National Allied Publications, Detective Comics, Inc., and related corporate names.  The list does not include trade paperbacks or series that included only reprints of previously published material.

List of DC imprint reprint collections | This is a list of DC Comics imprint reprint collections including trade paperbacks, hardcovers, and other special format collections.  DC Comics is one of the largest comic book and graphic novel publishers in North America.  They have published comics under a number of different imprints and corporate names.  The collections on this page include all series, mini-series, limited series, and graphic novels published under the imprints All-Star, Johnny DC, Tangent, and those Amalgam Comics published by DC.

Amalgam Comics | Amalgam Comics was a publishing imprint shared by DC Comics and Marvel Comics, in which the two comic book publishers merged their characters into new ones (e.g., DC Comics' Batman and Marvel Comics' Wolverine became the Amalgam character Dark Claw).  These characters first appeared in a series of twelve comic books which were published in 1996, between the third and fourth issues of the "DC vs. Marvel" miniseries.  A second set of twelve comic books followed one year later.

Azrael (comic book) | Azrael was an American comic book ongoing series, published by DC Comics based on the character Azrael.  The name, inspired by the Islamic Angel of Death, is primarily associated with two characters: Jean-Paul Valley and Michael Lane.  Valley was primarily featured between 1992 and 2003, while Lane was the star of a comics series which ran from 2009 to 2011.

DC Universe Presents Batman Superman | DC Universe Presents Batman Superman was part of the UK 'Collector's Edition' line of DC Comics published by Titan Magazines.  It was published between September 2007 and March 2008.  It reprinted comics from DC Comics from the United States relating to Batman, Superman or the DC Universe in general.

response.summary_2: Jean Loring is a fictional character in comic books published by DC Comics.
